Title: Explain this so I can decide
Prompt: Explain [topic] to me like I have 10 minutes and need to make a decision, not become an expert. Include the 2-3 things most people get wrong about it.
When to use: You need a fast, decision-ready grip on an unfamiliar topic.

Title: Get meeting-ready on a topic
Prompt: I need to get up to speed on [topic/competitor/trend] for a meeting. Give me the need-to-know facts, the current debate or open question in the space, and 3 smart questions I could ask.
When to use: Walking into a meeting on something you don't know cold.

Title: Compare two options
Prompt: I'm deciding between [option A] and [option B] for [purpose]. Lay out the tradeoffs in a simple table: cost, effort, risk, and who each option is better for. Don't just pick one - help me see the decision clearly.
When to use: Weighing a choice and want the tradeoffs laid out cleanly.

Title: Stress-test a claim before I repeat it
Prompt: I've heard that [claim/assumption]. Before I repeat this to my team, stress-test it: what would have to be true for this to be right, and what's the most likely way it's wrong or oversimplified?
When to use: Before you pass along something you're not fully sure of.

Title: Draft an email
Prompt: Draft an email to [audience] about [topic]. The goal is [what you want them to do or know]. Tone: [professional / friendly / urgent]. Keep it under [150] words. Context: [paste background, prior thread, key facts].
When to use: Any routine email you'd rather not start from a blank page.

Title: Say no gracefully
Prompt: I need to say no to [request] without damaging the relationship. Draft a short, respectful reply that explains why, offers an alternative if there is one, and ends on a positive note.
When to use: Turning down a request while keeping the relationship intact.

Title: Deliver difficult news
Prompt: I need to deliver difficult news to [audience] about [situation]. Help me draft this so it's honest and clear but doesn't create panic or bury the real message. Tone: calm and direct.
When to use: Sensitive messages where wording really matters.

Title: Turn notes into a follow-up email
Prompt: Turn these raw meeting notes into a follow-up email with next steps and owners: [paste notes]. Keep it skimmable - bold the deadlines.
When to use: Right after a meeting, to send actions before they're forgotten.

Title: Generate a range of approaches
Prompt: I'm trying to solve this problem: [describe it]. Give me 8 different approaches, ranging from safe/incremental to bold/unconventional. For each, note one risk and one reason it might work.
When to use: Opening up options when you're anchored on one idea.

Title: Devil's advocate on my plan
Prompt: Play devil's advocate on this plan: [describe your plan]. What's the strongest argument against it? What would have to be true for it to fail?
When to use: Pressure-testing a plan before you commit to it.

Title: Narrow a list of ideas
Prompt: Here are the ideas I'm considering: [list them]. Help me narrow to the top 3 using these criteria: [e.g., cost, speed, member impact]. Show your reasoning, not just a ranking.
When to use: Too many options and you need to get to a shortlist.

Title: Get unstuck with questions
Prompt: I'm stuck on [problem] and keep going in circles. Ask me 5 questions that might reveal what I'm missing - don't give me the answer yet.
When to use: When you're spinning and can't see what you're missing.

Title: Skeptical reviewer pass
Prompt: Review this [email/deck/document] as if you were a skeptical [manager/customer/reviewer]. What's unclear, what's missing, and where would someone push back? Don't rewrite it yet - just tell me what's weak.
When to use: Before sending something important up or out.

Title: Tighten without losing meaning
Prompt: Tighten this without losing meaning. Cut anything that doesn't change the reader's decision. [Paste text]
When to use: When a draft is right but too long or wordy.

Title: Consistency check
Prompt: Check this for consistency: are the numbers, terms, and claims the same throughout? Flag anything that contradicts itself or seems inconsistent with the rest. [Paste document]
When to use: Long documents where details can drift out of sync.

Title: Re-aim for a different audience
Prompt: Here's a draft written for [original audience]: [paste]. Rewrite it for [different audience - e.g. frontline team members / an executive] - same facts, right level of detail and tone for them.
When to use: Reusing content for a different reader.

Title: Turn notes into a slide outline
Category: 5. Creating presentations, reports, or business documents
Prompt: I need to turn this into a slide outline: [paste raw content/notes]. Give me a title and one governing thought per slide, in the order that tells the clearest story - not just the order I gave you the information.
When to use: Starting a deck from messy raw material.

Title: Turn bullets into clean paragraphs
Category: 5. Creating presentations, reports, or business documents
Prompt: Turn these bullet points into 3 tight paragraphs for a business document. Plain language, no jargon, active voice. [Paste bullets]
When to use: Converting rough bullets into finished prose.

Title: Write an executive summary
Category: 5. Creating presentations, reports, or business documents
Prompt: Here's my full [report/analysis]: [paste or describe]. Write a half-page executive summary: what's the situation, what's the recommendation, what do you need from the reader.
When to use: Topping a long document with a summary leaders will read.

Title: Find the so-what
Category: 5. Creating presentations, reports, or business documents
Prompt: Look at this slide/section: [paste]. What's the 'so what' - the one sentence a busy exec would actually remember? If it's not obvious, tell me what's missing.
When to use: When a slide has content but no clear point.

Title: Summarize into takeaways and actions
Prompt: Summarize the following into: (1) the 3 most important takeaways, (2) any decisions made, (3) action items with owners if mentioned. Keep it to half a page. [Paste notes, transcript, or document]
When to use: Turning long material into something usable in minutes.

Title: Pre-read a long document
Prompt: I'm about to read [a long document/report]. Give me a 5-bullet summary of what it covers so I know what to look for, then I'll ask follow-up questions.
When to use: Deciding what to focus on before you dive in.

Title: Reconcile multiple documents
Prompt: I have [2-3] documents/reports on the same topic: [paste or describe each]. Summarize where they agree, where they disagree, and what's still unclear across all of them.
When to use: Making sense of several sources on the same subject.

Title: Find patterns to sanity-check
Prompt: Here's a data set [paste table or describe it]. What are the 3 most interesting patterns, and what's one explanation for each that I should sanity-check before presenting it?
When to use: A first read on data before you build the story.

Title: Explain surprising numbers
Prompt: I have numbers that look [surprising/concerning/promising]: [paste them]. What are 3 possible explanations, ranked by how likely they are, and what would I need to check to tell them apart?
When to use: When the data does something you didn't expect.

Title: Anticipate the question I'll get
Prompt: I need to present this data [paste/describe] in a meeting tomorrow. What's the one question I'm most likely to get asked, and how should I answer it?
When to use: Prepping to present numbers you'll be challenged on.

Title: Build an Excel formula
Prompt: I need an Excel formula that does [describe what you want, e.g. 'flags any row where sales dropped more than 10% month over month']. Give me the formula, explain what each part does, and tell me what to double check.
When to use: When you know what you want but not the formula.

Title: Prep messy data for a pivot
Prompt: Here's a messy data table [paste or describe]. What's the cleanest way to organize this for a pivot table? What columns should I add or fix first?
When to use: Getting raw data ready to analyze.

Title: Template a repetitive task
Prompt: I do this task manually every [day/week]: [describe the repetitive task]. Help me figure out which parts could be templated or automated, and draft a reusable template or checklist for it.
When to use: A recurring task you'd like to stop redoing from scratch.

Title: Streamline a multi-step process
Prompt: Here's a multi-step process I follow: [describe the steps]. Where are the redundant or slow steps, and what's one change that would save the most time without adding risk?
When to use: Finding the waste in a process you own.

Title: Review code in plain language
Prompt: Review this code for readability and obvious bugs, not just whether it runs: [paste code]. Explain any issue in plain language, not just 'fix this line.'
When to use: Checking code you or someone else wrote.

Title: Write a script with test guidance
Prompt: I need a script/formula that does [describe the task]. Give me the code, explain what each part does, and tell me what to test before trusting it.
When to use: Getting a working script plus how to verify it.
